title: Chapter 1
html:
Foreword

Of all the works in Shakespeare’s canon, Troilus and Cressida stands as a singular affront to the expectations of comedy, tragedy, or history. It inhabits a zone of indeterminacy, an unstable mixture of satire and despair, where laughter curdles into disillusion and grandeur collapses under the weight of irony. Written around 1601–1602, at the very center of Shakespeare’s creative maturity, this play emerges not as one of his more frequently celebrated masterpieces but as a disturbing experiment whose afterlife has perplexed audiences and scholars alike. To encounter it is to stumble upon Shakespeare at his most corrosive, his most unillusioned about the heroic myths of antiquity, and his most modern in a bleakness that anticipates Swift, Nietzsche, and Beckett. Its existence alone compels us to reconsider what we mean when we speak of the Shakespearean canon, for it refuses every generic resolution and mocks the very impulses toward meaning that tragedy and comedy usually sanction.

The historical context of the play situates it between the triumphs of the great tragedies and the consolations of the late romances. England at the turn of the seventeenth century was a nation torn between Elizabethan grandeur and Jacobean uncertainty: the queen’s reign ending, succession anxieties mounting, the promise of national destiny shadowed by doubts concerning continuity, honor, and trust. To choose the Trojan War as a subject at such a moment was to expose the rift between mythic past and disenchanted present. Unlike Homer, who sings of wrath and heroic glory, or Chaucer, who elegizes doomed love with medieval delicacy, Shakespeare selects degradation as his keynote. Heroes are debunked, lovers betrayed, philosophers shown ridiculous, and even time itself seems less the healer of wounds than a corrosive solvent that eats away the possibility of permanence. If Hamlet dramatizes uncertainty and King Lear dramatizes suffering, then Troilus and Cressida dramatizes futility.

What most scandalizes in this play is its refusal of transcendence. In Shakespeare’s hands, the Trojan War is no longer the sublime stage for Achilles’ wrath or Hector’s nobility; it is a squalid carnival of ego, desire, and venality. The great figures of antiquity, refracted through Renaissance humanism, are revealed as grotesques: Ulysses a manipulative rhetorician, Achilles a sulking brute, Hector noble yet tragically duped, Troilus himself blinded by idealism, and Cressida, the most maligned of Shakespeare’s heroines, made emblem of mutability. In its merciless exposure, the play echoes Montaigne’s skepticism more than Homer’s or Virgil’s grandeur. If the tragedies wrestle with the gods of destiny and the comedies with the gods of love, then Troilus and Cressida finds no gods at all, only men and women stripped of divine sanction, locked in a comedy too bitter to redeem itself and a tragedy too absurd to inspire awe.

It is therefore no surprise that the play was received with unease even in Shakespeare’s own time. Its publication history—appearing in quarto without a clear performance record, framed in an epistle that equivocates about its nature—suggests embarrassment as well as intrigue. To label it a comedy, as one edition did, is to ignore its corrosive despair; to call it a tragedy is to overlook its savage laughter; to categorize it as a history is to betray its refusal of patriotic coherence. Troilus and Cressida is a play that exposes the inadequacy of categories themselves, and in doing so, Shakespeare announces his indifference to critical taxonomy. The play seems less performed than deferred, less enjoyed than endured, its status in the canon forever that of the disturbing guest who cannot be expelled.

Yet this disturbance is precisely its significance. The Shakespearean imagination, so often celebrated for its capaciousness, here turns to the anatomy of disillusion. One feels in every line the dramatist’s genius for rhetoric and character, but also his desire to annihilate the very ideals his words once exalted. It is as though the creator of Falstaff, Hamlet, and Lear now turns his gaze upon the very myths of civilization—the Homeric foundation-stories of Europe—and strips them bare. Shakespeare anticipates Nietzsche’s genealogical unmaskings, showing how honor disguises appetite, how rhetoric displaces truth, how love collapses into exchange, how greatness is only the performance of power. Troilus and Cressida is not a play about war, but about the ruin of meaning itself under the pressures of desire and cunning.

In this, Shakespeare reveals his philosophical depth. We are accustomed to regard him as the supreme dramatist, but here he emerges as a thinker who rivals Machiavelli in cynicism and Montaigne in doubt. The speeches of Ulysses, though seductive in their eloquence, expose the terrifying possibility that order is but a fragile contrivance, that hierarchy is always at risk of collapse. Troilus’s idealism, so fervent, becomes a parody of fidelity, as if Shakespeare wished to suggest that eros itself is inseparable from self-deception. And Cressida, vilified by tradition, is not so much a figure of treachery as of necessity, bound to betray because the world itself permits no constancy. Each figure becomes emblem of a fallen condition, where no Platonic ideal endures, and the chivalric dream dissolves into farce.

Stylistically, the play displays Shakespeare’s brilliance in its harshest register. Gone are the lyrical effusions of the sonnets, the soaring cadences of Romeo and Juliet, or the vast symphonic harmonies of Hamlet. In their place we encounter jagged exchanges, corrosive wit, a verbal economy that delights in cutting down pretensions. The language itself seems weaponized, each phrase turned against the very notion of beauty or nobility. Shakespeare here anticipates the satirists of later ages—Dryden, Swift, Pope—yet surpasses them in his ability to make satire itself tragic. There is little sweetness in this style, but there is grandeur in its refusal of consolation. Even when the verse falters into prose, it is the faltering of a master dramatist who knows how to make disjunctions speak as eloquently as harmonies.

The originality of Troilus and Cressida cannot be overstated. To take the most venerated of Western myths—the Trojan War—and reduce it to the theater of absurd ambition was a boldness unparalleled. Homer ennobled the quarrel of Achilles and Agamemnon; Virgil sanctified the Trojan fall as prologue to empire; medieval romance softened the tale into courtly love; Chaucer turned it into a meditation on desire’s mutability. Shakespeare alone renders the whole fabric corrosive, laughing not with joy but with contempt, and in that contempt discovering a new form of dramatic power. It is no accident that modern critics have recognized in this play affinities with Brecht and Beckett: the disjunctions, the alienation, the sense that the world’s scripts are already bankrupt and the actors caught in a farce without end. Shakespeare, three centuries earlier, had already dramatized the abyss.

Comparisons to his contemporaries only underscore his audacity. Marlowe, had he lived, might have exalted Achilles’ wrath into cosmic defiance; Jonson would have moralized the follies of the Greeks; Chapman, translator of Homer, sought to preserve the grandeur of antiquity. Shakespeare instead dismantles the entire edifice. He is closer here to Dante in his bitter realism, or to Cervantes in his exposure of illusions, though even Cervantes permits a gentleness foreign to this play. One could say that Troilus and Cressida is Shakespeare’s anti-epic, his refusal of the sublime, his declaration that beneath the gilded pageantry of legend lies only the grotesque comedy of human appetite. It is as if he wished to remind us that literature itself, however splendid, is powerless to transfigure corruption.

And yet the play endures, not despite its corrosiveness but because of it. For in our own modernity—after the devastations of history, after the collapse of grand narratives, after the exposure of power’s hypocrisies—we recognize ourselves in its bleak laughter. What Shakespeare intuited four centuries ago we have lived in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries: that wars are fought not for ideals but for vanities, that love falters in the face of time, that language itself is endlessly manipulable, and that the human comedy is more savage than redemptive. To read or stage Troilus and Cressida today is to encounter the prophetic Shakespeare, the one who foresaw modern disenchantment. It speaks not to the Elizabethan court or the London stage alone, but to any age that has seen its ideals collapse into parody.

The endurance of this play within the Western canon owes not to popularity but to necessity. It is the necessary counterweight to Shakespeare’s idealisms, the acid that prevents the sweetness of his comedies or the sublimities of his tragedies from cloying. Without it, the canon would lack the depth of disillusion that makes affirmation credible. Just as Dante required the Inferno before the Paradiso, so Shakespeare required this play to confront disillusion before permitting himself the fragile reconciliations of the late romances. Troilus and Cressida is his Inferno, his vision of a world where love is a contract, war a charade, and meaning itself an illusion. Its laughter is the laughter of the abyss, but precisely for that reason, it must be heard.

To praise Shakespeare’s stylistic genius here is to praise his courage in refusing to console. The density of the verse, the bitter eloquence of the speeches, the refusal to ornament what is corrupt—all reveal an artist at the height of his powers yet willing to estrange himself from audience and posterity. The originality of this stance cannot be assimilated; it remains a challenge to every interpreter. One can admire the play, but one cannot love it as one loves Twelfth Night or A Midsummer Night’s Dream. Its greatness is that of Goya’s Black Paintings, or Swift’s Tale of a Tub, or Beckett’s Endgame: corrosive, necessary, eternal.

If the canon is that which must be preserved against forgetfulness, then Troilus and Cressida is indispensable precisely because it refuses the comforts of memory. It reminds us that literature is not only the guardian of ideals but the exposer of delusions. Shakespeare, more than any other writer, understood that human beings live by fictions, and that to dismantle those fictions is as important as to create them. In this play, he dismantles with unmatched ferocity, leaving us with a vision of the human condition that is as bleak as it is truthful. To read it is to be stripped of illusions; to stage it is to confront the grotesque comedy of existence; to preserve it in the canon is to acknowledge that without disillusion, no affirmation is worth having.

Thus Troilus and Cressida, the unloved child of Shakespeare’s genius, persists as one of his most indispensable works. Its endurance is not in applause but in necessity, not in delight but in revelation. If we are to understand Shakespeare not only as the poet of love, ambition, and fate but also as the anatomist of disillusion, we must turn to this play. In its corrosive laughter, we hear the premonition of modernity; in its refusal of transcendence, we glimpse the abyss of human striving; and in its very bitterness, we discover a new form of greatness. To preserve this play in the canon is to confess that literature must tell us not only what we wish to hear, but also what we most dread to know.
